summary: This post demonstrates how to use TLA+ to model and verify a zero-downtime deployment system. Starting with a naive approach that immediately fails verification, Wayne iteratively builds a correct specification by adding a load balancer, update flags, and a coordinator process. The model checker exhaustively explores all possible orderings of concurrent server updates to find bugs. The final spec ensures both safety properties: at least one server is always accessible, and all accessible servers run the same code version.
key_points: TLA+ model checking exhaustively explores all possible orderings of concurrent operations - even 3 servers can have 90+ behaviors to verify
The naive deployment approach fails immediately because servers update at different rates, breaking version consistency
Adding a load balancer and coordinator process enables safe rolling updates by controlling which servers are accessible during updates
Temporal properties can verify system-wide conditions like 'when done, all servers must be back in the load balancer'
The spec can be extended to model failures, blue-green deployments, minimum capacity requirements, and lossy communication
money_quotes: As with any complicated system, it makes sense for us to design it up from, and then check the design itself for bugs.
We're done! Except that deployment model is horribly broken.
Instead of 6 behaviors and 18 steps, we have up to 90 behaviors and 1170 steps.
'But wait!' you've probably said, because you're a programmer and used to seeing things go horribly wrong.
Just kidding. We can totally check this in our model.
TLA+ has the power and flexibility to model all of these cases.
key_insight: Formal specification lets you find concurrency bugs in deployment systems before writing any code by exhaustively checking all possible orderings of operations.
